True horror buff here, went to see it the first day. Is it as ¨good¨ as the original? Um,,,no. However, you really can´t/shouldn´t try to view it through that lens. Watching it and comparing it to the first film isn´t fair, or reasonable, and will leave you disappointed. The original coming out in 1973 was groundbreaking, there had never been anything like it seen before. The dialog, the special effects then, the profanity, the story of demonic possession, it was just shocking and unheard off. People were caught completely off guard, so the impact was staggering (fainting, heart attacks, women going into labor in theaters) What´s going to match something like that today (?) Nothing. It´s 2023. ¨Chucky¨, ¨Annabelle¨, ¨The Conjuring¨ (and so on) people have now ¨seen it all¨. The over the top gore of films like Saw, Terrifier, have tainted us, that, along with few original ideas, there´s no shock value left. Keeping expectations in prospective, and accepting it for what it was, although somewhat disjointed in places, I enjoyed it, and I believe most fans of the original will also. There´s also a nice surprise at the end to set up the second chapter of the trilogy. Go see it, you know you want to, ¨THE POWER OF CHRIST COMPELS YOU!¨

I was 5 yrs old, when my older cousins took me to see the original, at the Cleveland Park  theaters (now called Cineplex-something or other) in Wash/DC, my hometown. Cleveland Park, is a very old, upper-NorthWest neighborhood in DC..and its walking distance to Georgetown Univ. Lets just say it has an "affect" to it, that lends itself to the creepiness of the house that the Exorcist was filmed in, on the campus of the school. After seeing "The Exorcist", I couldnt sleep in my room for almost 2 weeks. I slept with my folks. But I also became a horror movie fan because I saw The Exorcist. I've seen the movie it was okay so far until the point where we get to the woman from the original movie talking to one of the fathers and she out right said the reason why she wasn't in the same room with the two priests in the original movie because "she wasn't part of their patriarchy". Wtf does that have to do with sexism? Was this movie made by people who never watched the original movie or hated it?! The two priests from the original movie wanted her out of the room just to keep her life and sanity safe! The two freaking priests ended up sacrificing their lives trying to save her daughter; if she was in the same room with them, she would've died as well or her daughter would die without the priests' help! And this is what she repays those heros?! By calling them "being sexist"?! Wtf?! The people who made this movie literally wrote her to be ungrateful!

After that, movie started going downwards as it continues to give religion in general the middle finger by killing off the random professional Exorcist, who wanted to help, but spared the idiots within the movie who are not professional Exorcists nor Exorcists in general; doing something like that without training is extremely dangerous! And the way they sacrifice one daughter for the other was so stupid; if they wanted both the girls safe, one of the adults should've sacrifice their own life to save both of them; but we didn't get that. It was a disappointment.

Too young to watch the original when it came out but did read the book, The Exorist, at the curious age of 11. And was utterly riveted by the read. I eventually watched the film some years later and found its low-key presentation to be an utterly spooky outing. The BBC this week, celebrating its 50th anniversary, aired The Exorcist. The film remains timeless and as creepy as it was back then. (A family member texted: "watched it at 1am and still gives me goosebumps"). For me, no horror film since comes close. This latest attempt of a reboot  I suspect will remain without impact. Subtlety in the spooky storytelling in modern day dramas is lost, as a defeaning soundtrack, a chorus of "who-can-scream-the-loudest" and a predictable flashing lightbulb etc. as if begging the audience to jump out of their seat, takes its place. All resulting in a constant reminder that this is just a work of...fiction, And tonight's sleep will be sound.  Simplicity is sadly lost on modern-day horror writers and so, for me, I know this film will come, go and remain unremembered. And to all real-life exorcists out there:  it will be just another OTT horror flick.

I watched 'The Exorcist ' for the first time time in 1974 at the Odeon cinema in London's Leicester Square, and afterwards I was terrified at the thought of having to travel across London late at night to reach my hotel. I have never seen a more scary movie than this. I was only 19 at the time. Even now, in 2023, it's not shown on British tv without serious censorship, 50 years later. This new sequel opens at my local cinema today - I'm not so readily shocked these days ! It will be interesting though to see how it fares in comparison to the original.

I think these trailers are too long and showing too much.  Just personal opinion. I’m a big believer in fear of the unknown being the most effective way to craft a horror marketing campaign. Seeing and hearing the girl characters so much after full possession is very desensitizing.  instead of wondering “how far will these transformations go?” Now the only real questions are “how did we get here?”

I will be so disappointed if it ends up being a cult or a coven of witches.  That is so played out.

The “one gets to live” line also already leads me to believe this is retreading old turf—that demons possess people to break the faith of everyone in their vicinity.  But now it seems like it’s the same concept with a twist of “double possession.”  Like “what benevolent God would allow you to make such a terrible choice?”

I HOPE I’m surprised when this comes out, but so far I’m in the boat of I’m def going to see it, but my expectations are super low minus the iconic Ellen Burstyn returning.  I hope they wrote her a BIG ASS check for her time.

I'll watch it, but I doubt it will be any good? William Peter Blatty's 1971 novel gave me nightmares aged 13 in 1990 - I never saw to film until it was re-released in the cinema about 1998 in the UK - but back then it was, as a film considered one of the best horrors ever. My Mum saw it in the 70s at the pictures and said some people left early and that the St Johns Ambulance attendants were present for people that got sick. In modern times we have all become desensitised to horror because of the internet and social media? Violence and gore in excess now is nothing compared to the human mind, but when the original was released more people attended church and were probably more God and Satan fearing? 
Over the years Exorcist sequels and prequels have appeared that - to me - are nothing compared to the original, Mike Oldfield's Tubular Bells still gives me tingles down the spine with its opening, so what music in this will either be original or remixed?
I'm sorry to say that I think this film won't do that well at the cinema. I find that with the horror genre that 'Less is More' as the films that have really frightened me over the years have been ones that make you think more and don't rely upon blood and gore!
I highly recommend that people read The Exorcist by William Peter Blatty and Jaws by Peter Blenchley, Jaws as a film relied on suspense and the soundtrack in away that people remember the shower seen in Hitchcock's Psycho...

Has anyone stopped to consider just how many Exorcism movies have come out over the years? Seeing as the word exorcism is one of those tricky words, that while tied to a horror classic, is not bound to that film or even franchise (if indeed there ever really was one), but its marketability most certainly is linked. I do think the second Exorcism film was an actual true sequel, but as for the third, it was a script that was not originally intended to be an Exorcism movie, they simply rebranded it to boost ticket sales (if I remember correctly). That's common though in Hollywood. What I'm getting at is that you can slap the name Exorcism or The Exorcist on anything, because you can't exactly trademark something like The Plumber or The Policeman, they're professions and Exorcism is a... spiritual/psychological condition 🤣 Red Letter Media actually brought up this conversation is video not so long ago and put it more eloquently with their watchbait video. I'll link it, it's worth a watch just for the good times alone. 
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UeMadjM0ZZI
13:45 is about where they get into the topic of Exorcism and my God is it farking ridonkulous. I'm bringing this up because I'm honestly skeptical of another soulless, unimaginable knockoff. Oh, look! Now there's two kids possessed! AH HA! No deeper meanings, philosophies or pesky symbolisms apply here, just your hard-earned paycheck for the terror experience you'll forget moments after walking out of the theater in search of a urinal having chugged the largest Coke you've ever seen. I digress, what the hell were we talking about, again? I dunno, I have to piss.

The Exorcist is one my favorite movies of all time.  Watched it so many times.  And I love the BBC documentary on it too.  The first few seconds of the trailer rather than reminding me of the original I thought of Repossessed, the parody that Linda Blair did later on.  All the sequels, 2 which was unintentionally funny or 3 which was just plain boring or the sorta sequels hold a candle to the first one.  It's like the Halloween movie and it's zillion sequels, as far as I'm concerned there is still only one movie that exists in that universe.  The original.  Same goes for The Exorcist.  Bringing back Ellen Burstyn is a nice casting choice I guess but I doubt it will help.  Linda was right in not returning.  At least we see that the evil believes in equal opportunity possession.  I'll end up watching it when it comes on streaming somewhere.  But I doubt I'll be impressed.

It seems to me that nowadays they don't know to make the watcher feel tension, they don't know how to build up a scene slowly, without someone screaming or big noises. 

Imagine a slow scene, on a hall, where we can actually see our protagonist and get glimpses from someone down the hall moving like regan did on the first one (spider like at the stairs), but we just get glimpses. The lights start to turn off, but we're not getting a jump scare, no, we can actually see how the girl is hunting our protagonist, we can hear some weird sounds coming from the girl (not human vocal sounds because we know how that sounds and it is more disturbing to listening to somwthing "unknown"). Then, we can get a take from yellow eyes staring to the camera, everything is dark but the eyes, not pitch black so we can see the eyes and a bit of the face, as the girl says "God is not heeeeeeeeeeeeeere" and the last word fades into multi laughing people, all the lights start to flicker, we hear old school violin sounds from older films, the windows start to bleed a darkish goo and right there, when the tension rises, without any big noises or screams, we are back on the hall, everything seems normal, our protagonist checks herself if nothing is wrong while she acts completely freaked out and traumatized. When the protagonist gets up shaken and ready to flee, there she is, the girl looking "normal" standing at the end of the hall (kinda like the twins from the shining) staring back at out protagonist. 

Our protagonist starts walking quickly the other way, and the camera follows her but in a way that we can see what's behind her, and with every turn she makes, the girl appesrs following exactly behind her, walking normally but it wouldnt be possible because she was at least 10 meters away when we first saw her standing. This happens at least 3 times until our protagonist gets out of the apartment complex and she looks back. All windows are pitch black until one of the windows turns on in the top floor, revealing to us the girl and with her there is a kid. She looks back at the kid and starts eating eat him (like the possessed serpent lady from a movie that eats a girl in a cave). The camera at this point should be faraway so we dont see the CGI but just the image of the girl doing it. 

Our protagonist gets even more traumatized with eyes wide open, the camera zooms on her face and without and next second, its the same face of our protagonist in a mental asylum.

*SPOILER WARNING*

Universal Pictures had a test screening about 14 days ago &  I was there.  People are going to be so shocked at how GREAT this movie is.  The trailers don't really show you the MOVIE at all.  It's got so many layers and so many storylines (the Haitian Dad's story is really emotional & powerful, then there is Ann Dowd's story & Chris McNeil's story) --all of them are really interesting--so by the time these girls are in full throttle EXORCIST mode, you're so deeply invested & on the edge of your seat.  This is really a Psychological horror.  And of course having Ellen Burstyn and Linda Blair (Regan) back on screen greatly enhances the experience.  It's pretty clear that the next one (DECEIVER) is going to focus on Linda Blair as she will want to get revenge for her Mom.  Not saying the Mom dies.  But this is far & away the BEST sequel of the Exorcist (much stronger & more intense than Exorcist 3, which I loved) and it's also a direct sequel.  People at the test screening were running up the aisles screaming and one lady even passed out.   But be warned, the movie is very graphic.  I would not let my kids watch this until at least 16 or 17.

So remember how the woman in Hereditary tricks the family into the satanic cult? She’s in The Exorcist as a nurse and I think she’s doing something similar with the girls who are possessed. Her goal is to bring back the mother in the original movie and avenge the spirit. So as the nurse she tells the family that she knows someone who’s seen things like this before and that’s how she is perceived as someone who wants to help and has no bad intentions.

That’s why in the original trailer the spirit refers to the woman as “mother” in a sarcastic tone. In another part the nurse says that their hearts are beating in sync, but she knows that her trick is working and she’ll be able to avenge the spirit from the mother. That’s why the possessed girl says in the trailer that God played a trick on them

What bothers/scares me the most is that Ellen Burnstyn agreed to be part of this film...at all. Money has its allure, but there are parts of your sunset years you really don't want to mess with. This movie created so many problems for her, both professionally and personally.  The director must have promised her complete non-exposure to much of what ultimately is seen on the screen; with filming technology, she wouldn't have to be in the same room as the girls in possession make-up, etc. Watch the trailer again and see for yourself; her jump scares are on cue, not as true reactions. That's fine...i don't blame her one bit, however, I hope this doesn't end up causing her more problems. 
All that said, this movie has typical cliches of sequels to "great films." For example: oh, you thought one girl being possessed was scary and terrifying? This film will have FOUR! And, to up the stakes, the "good character" will have to decide who lives, and who gets taken to Hell! It's a horrible premise that was jotted down over coffee with a couple idiots rich enough to buy the rights. Now, you want to see how a sequel is SUPPOSED to be done? Watch the new Godzilla Minus One trailer. It captures the original flavor and deepens it with available CGI while adding historical context. The only thing that slightly bothers me is the inclusion of "other cultures exorcisms". First of that's an oxymoron. Exorcism was a word and a ritual coined by the Christians. No need for discount "diversity" in a movie like this. The fact is, save for possibly some African religions, no religions that were not Abrahamic, even had apotropaic rituals. The concept of banishment, exorcism, protection from evil was introduced to human civilization through these religions. Pagan religions didn't have a concept of good vs evil, but rather good and evil at the same time. They appeased their deities and spirits when needed, or sought protection from one by bribing the other to fight it (basically  stirring sh@t on a spiritual level), but they *did not* have anything even alike to the ritual of exorcism of evil. The original movies respected this accurate fact of history. 
I'd hate to see a buddhist monk smacking a singing bowl over the girls, or a viking draving "protective bindrunes" on the girls and calling that an "exorcism". 
P.S. No, what shamans did was not exorcism either, nor strictly apotropaic. Not even Frazer argued that.

Why?  The emergence of a sequel to the iconic 1973 film, "The Exorcist," can be viewed as a contemporary endeavor, one that appears primarily motivated by financial gain at the box office. It seems to cater to the sensibilities of a modern audience, potentially disconnected from the nuances of cinematic excellence that characterized its predecessor. In essence, this production may rely considerably on the allure of a well-known title, underscoring the prevailing trend of leveraging established names in the industry to drive commercial success. However, the discerning viewer may find themselves yearning for the timeless qualities of cinematic storytelling that once graced the silver screen, lamenting a departure from the artistry that once defined the realm of filmmaking.

The Exorcist has terrified audiences since 1973, but few know the story is true; evidence exposes shocking secrets about the real-life exorcism of a 13-year-old boy, which inspired the iconic horror film. The Exorcist true story is based on the experience of Roland Doe. In March 1949, newspapers reported that a 14-year-old boy known as “Robbie” or “Roland Doe” was possessed by something sinister and that The Exorcist's priests performed an exorcism on him.

This looks like every other generic demonic possession movie from the past 20 years. The Exorcist will never be replicated for a number of reasons. Friedkin never viewed his film as a horror picture but rather a family drama with horror elements. The characters were the primary focus and they were well written and relatable. The horror came from Chris McNeil and Damian Karras who are both suffering from a crisis of faith ( Chris in medical institutions and Karras in religion as a  whole) coming in contact with something completely alien that forces them both to re-evaluate their belief systems. The pea soup and head spinning is just the catalyst rather than the main focus and there in lies the issue with this film and the majority of films like it. The horror element is the main focus and takes priority over character development.  It's more about creepy imagery and jump scares than themes. The only film that has come close to matching the quality of the original is Exorcist 3 and Blattys writing and direction make all the difference.

Still probably enough ghost believers for the moment, plus the U.S. has an influx of Muslims who may also get shocked by ghosts, but I wouldn’t put many more religious thrillers on the production schedule. Though there’s probably enough of a population of dunderheads who just believe in generic ghosts and sprits for awhile.
